mnemonics.title: DEADLOCK Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: D.E.A.D.L.O.C.K: Deadlock requires Four conditions - Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait. Remember: 'Deadlock Employees Are Desperate: Leave Office Collect Knowledge'

mnemonics.title: Readers-Writers Priority
mnemonics.content: R.W.P: Reader-Writer Problem - 'Readers Relax while Writers Wait' (Writer priority) vs 'Readers Run while Writers Weep' (Reader priority)

mnemonics.title: File Lock Types
mnemonics.content: S.E. Lock: Shared (many can read) - Exclusive (only one can write)

acronyms.usage: A technique where modifications create private copies, preserving original data for other processes sharing the file.
Used in lock queuing to ensure fair access - processes waiting longest get lock first.
A distributed file system protocol that enables file sharing across network by mounting remote directories locally.
File system that manages files across multiple networked servers, enabling file sharing across an organization.
A flag in open() that prevents creation if file already exists, used for atomic file creation.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a library: Readers sit quietly and multiple can read together. Writers need a private room alone. Writers wait if readers are reading; new readers wait if a writer is waiting (writer priority).
Two people trying to pass each other in a hallway - Person A moves left, Person B moves right, both block. Neither can proceed without the other moving back.
Think of a shared document on Google Docs - initially everyone sees same view. When someone edits, they get their own private copy to work on, while others see the original.

analogies.analogy: Think of a restroom stall - when occupied (locked), others must wait. Exclusive lock is like a single-occupancy bathroom. Shared lock is like multiple people being allowed in a dressing room to try on clothes together.
Hotel room checkout - you have the room until checkout time (lease expiry), after which the hotel can give it to another guest.
Two people trying to update the same Google Sheet cell at the exact same time - whoever's update reaches the server last overwrites the first person's changes.
Advisory is like a 'Quiet Hours' sign - people can choose to follow it. Mandatory is like an actual rule enforced by security - you're physically stopped if you violate it.
